author: @DavidKenny64
text: Click Bait!  You only showed 6 ways to splice wires together?  Even after stating that there are 1235 ways? ;)

author: @karlmyers6518
text: I recently came across wago connectors and wish I had them years ago but for tidying I prefer solder and heat shrink.  Thanks mate

author: @mysterytechknowledge3664
text: When using a Wire Nut, test the connection by holding the wire nut with one hand, then pull firmly on each of the other wires. If any wires move, remove the nut and realign the wires and try again.

author: @omna1
text: I think the smoosh teqnique is the best but with a twist once the wires are together, heard somewhere that thats how nasa does their wires splicing and stuck with it ever since, all good though

replies.author: @brendancull8316
replies.text: I was taught to solder while working as an Aircraft Electrical Technician in the RAF, and trained to Military Specification. I find it hard to believe that NASA would use this smoosh technique, as I have never seen anything quite so abysmal ever.

author: @docink6175
text: I don't always have access to a soldering gun / iron or heat shrink tubing for that matter. So I will cover the twisted splice with engine silicone.. I've always called the smooshed connection an interconnected or woven connection. While the woven connection is strong if soldered it doesn't work well in the field. If I use wire nuts I will also use a little bit of silicone to seal the end from any moisture...

author: @MrGeekGamer
text: Remember to always put the heat shrink on on of the leads first. I never do :D

replies.author: @mysterytechknowledge3664
replies.text: Solder twice, Heat-shrink once! (Also my not-preferred but most common method.)

author: @Bnryzombie
text: number 3 is the worst way to join two wires together with solder. Number 2 is the best.

author: @onemansjunk01
text: Just a small favour if I may ask can you change the shade of blue in your opening titles it hard to see

author: @TheClumsyFairy
text:
To be honest with my eyes, you have accurately recreated what I see when I try and do stuff close up with small fiddly things (I am sure there's a rude joke to be had here, though I'll leave it to someone less tired than I am at the moment <grin>).

PS. "Crimpy tool" is the correct name for it.

author: @smob0
text:
If you plan on using wire nuts, be sure not to reuse them. If they need replacing, cut them off along with the wires inside. They bite into the wires as you tighten them on, and don't come off cleanly if installed properly, and even if you can get them off, they create cuts in the wire. 

Also if you are doing this with mains AC, you probably want to put a grounded metal box around such a connection. (This is building code for using wire nuts here in canada, and I imagine the US as well)

author: @timothygorman7528
text: I'm from the US, so yes, "Wire Nuts" - but it's unbelievable that you've "never seen them before" - ha ha - For we here, it's the cheapest, fastest, and best if temporary (but even some appliances and equipment come manufactured with "wire nuts."   Here it's like oxygen.  You'll find wire nuts in building electrical boxes, equipment like table saws, air conditioners, etc - which are much more permanent.  I guess they were more popular before modern day "terminal blocks" and things like this.   But WOW :)   And again, a great video Luke :)

author: @roberthopgood1894
text: Soldering is best for uses that will not be accessible afterward

author: @lightcapmath2777
text: nice to see the different ways to connect two wires together.

author: @beamer.electronics
text: Interesting, thank you. Though I did initially think that you'd discovered a natural mathematical rhythm to electrical splicing - Fibonacci Sequence: 0 1...1 2 3 5...8 13 21 etc., and that splicing was somehow connected with music, biology and the natural world ;)

author: @stevetobias4890
text: You forgot the Waco connectors. The "smoosh" connection, after pushing them together twist in opposite directions like you did with the 90 degree twist before soldering. It gives it a neater and cleaner finish.

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: I think you mean WAGO connectors, these are awesome connectors, they are so easy to use and rated for in wall use too.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @hootsmin yes was a typo, I'm typing with amputated fingers

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: @stevetobias4890 Try dipping wounds in 3-3.5% hydrogen peroxide and apply comfrey cream to minimise scarring.  I nearly sliced the tip of mine off last week too its healing nicely though.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @hootsmin this happened when I was 17 and was burnt to the bone after my first epileptic seizures into a campfire, that was in 1984. Only my left thumb is fully functional. Left 3 fingers only bend at knuckles right hand is half thumb and 3 stumps. 54 now but I have accomplished a lot in life. Oddly enough it's the best thing that could have happened considering the direction my life was headed.
